result:
Opening DB connection costs real time — TCP handshake, TLS, auth round-trips. Doing it per request burns latency and DB resources.

Pool keeps N connections already open, idle, ready.
App borrows one, runs query, returns it.
Next request reuses same socket — no handshake tax.

Knobs that matter:
min_size — connections held even when idle
max_size — ceiling before new requests wait or error
idle_timeout — close connections nobody touched
max_lifetime — force-recycle old connections (avoid stale TCP, rotate credentials, dodge DB-side timeouts)
acquire_timeout — how long caller waits before giving up

Sizing rule of thumb: max_size ≈ cores_on_db × small_factor, not "number of app threads." More connections ≠ more throughput — past a point DB spends time context-switching instead of working.

Gotchas:
Transactions pin connection for their duration — long transactions starve pool.
Prepared statements may be per-connection — cache invalidated on recycle.
Serverless/short-lived workers defeat pooling → use external pooler (PgBouncer, RDS Proxy).
One pool per process, not per request. Don't wrap pool in another pool.
Under load, exhausted pool manifests as latency spike, not error — monitor wait time, not just errors.
